conversations.title: Our Memory
conversations.subtitle: Section A, 1b and 1c

lines.speaker: peter
lines.text: Hi, Mum.

lines.speaker: mum
lines.text: Hi, Peter. What's up?

lines.speaker: peter
lines.text: I can't remember what things you asked me to buy.

lines.speaker: mum
lines.text: OK, so tell me what you've got so far.

lines.speaker: peter
lines.text: Let me see ... butter, milk, eggs, flour, ...

lines.speaker: mum
lines.text: Flour? That's not what I asked you to buy.

lines.speaker: peter
lines.text: Oh, OK. I'll put it back.

lines.speaker: mum
lines.text: Did you buy any sugar? It's running out.

lines.speaker: peter
lines.text: Right! I wonder how I forgot about the sugar.

lines.speaker: mum
lines.text: And don't forget to buy strawberries. I'm going to make a strawberry cake, remember?

lines.speaker: peter
lines.text: Oh, yeah! Anything else, Mum?

lines.speaker: mum
lines.text: One more thing, darling. Next time, take a shopping list!

conversations.instruction: Listen to Peter and his mum. What was Peter asked to buy, and what did he forget?

questions.prompt: What did Peter wrongly put in his basket?
What was running out at home?
What is Mum going to make?

conversations.title: Our Memory
conversations.subtitle: Section A, 1d and 1e

lines.speaker: billy
lines.text: Morning, Fu Xing. Sorry I forgot your birthday yesterday.

lines.speaker: fu_xing
lines.text: Oh, that's OK, Billy. But you're forgetting quite a lot recently. Is everything OK?

lines.speaker: billy
lines.text: Yes, fine, thanks. I don't know why I am so forgetful.

lines.speaker: fu_xing
lines.text: Maybe you're too busy. Can I help you with anything?

lines.speaker: billy
lines.text: Thanks. You are a good friend ... Oh no! I left my homework at home! I need to go back!

lines.speaker: fu_xing
lines.text: Go! I'll meet you at the school hall then.

lines.speaker: billy
lines.text: Right, Professor Green's lecture on how to improve your memory.

lines.speaker: fu_xing
lines.text: You'd better hurry up. It will begin at 9:00.

lines.speaker: billy
lines.text: Oh, no. I thought it was 9:30!

lines.speaker: fu_xing
lines.text: Oh Billy, you really should go to that lecture on time!

conversations.instruction: Listen to Billy and Fu Xing. What problems does Billy have?

questions.prompt: What time does Professor Green’s lecture begin?

conversations.title: Our Memory
conversations.subtitle: Section A, 2b and 2c

lines.speaker: billy
lines.text: Professor, I don't quite understand what you said about linking knowledge.

lines.speaker: professor
lines.text: Well, knowledge is like a chain, and new knowledge is best remembered by linking it with old knowledge. The more links you create, the more you remember.

lines.speaker: fu_xing
lines.text: Professor, I often get some words mixed up, like "kitchen" and "chicken".

lines.speaker: professor
lines.text: Pay attention to details. Study how they are different from each other. You can also create a picture for each word in your mind.

lines.speaker: peter
lines.text: Hello, Professor, could you tell me why it is much easier to forget things to buy than to forget how to ride a bike?

lines.speaker: professor
lines.text: Scientists are still working on this mystery. For one thing, the memory of how to ride a bike is probably stored in a more stable area of our brain.

lines.speaker: peter
lines.text: There are so many mysteries about our brain!

lines.speaker: professor
lines.text: Exactly! And at your age, your brain is developing fast, so put it to good use!

conversations.instruction: Listen to the lecture. What advice does the professor give about memory?

questions.prompt: How is new knowledge best remembered, according to the professor?
options: By linking it with old knowledge

questions.prompt: What example of mixed-up words does Fu Xing give?
Why might riding a bike be easier to remember than things to buy?
options: Bike memory may be stored in a more stable area of the brain

items.after: is like a chain, and new knowledge is best remembered by linking it with old knowledge.
. Study how they are different from each other.

items.before: Scientists are still working on this
items.after: . Such memory is probably stored in a more stable area of our brain.
